title: Anger at the Person You Were Before You Got Sick
dek: You look at old photographs and feel a sudden, violent surge of fury toward the woman who didn't know what was coming. This is not a moral failure; it is the biology of grief, and your body is demanding that you feel it.
bodyMarkdown:
## The Quiet Outrage of Remembering

It is 3:17 AM, the sky outside your window a bruised purple, and your chest is suddenly ablaze—not in the wild, reckless fire of rage aimed outward, but a slow, smoldering heat that gathers behind your sternum like a coal pressed just beneath skin and bone. Your diaphragm clenches unevenly, as if struggling to draw a full breath through a narrowing canyon. Somewhere deeper, past the familiar spasm of your masseter and the ever-tense trapezius knotting at your neck, the psoas muscle—your body’s primal, unacknowledged guardian—tightens. You feel it coil, a secret fist clenched inside your hips. It is this physical truth you cannot ignore: your anger is real and embodied, lodged in flesh and fascia, not mere thought or moral failing.

This is the anger at the person you were before your illness. The you who moved differently, who breathed more freely, who wielded your body as though it were a loyal tool instead of a fragile vessel. This anger rises unbidden, uninvited, often in the quiet hours when the world is still and your mind is the loudest company. It is not a betrayal of that past self, but a biological response to loss—the shattering of a familiar landscape, the grief that comes with the sudden narrowing of your physical world.

You were taught to see anger as something dangerous, something to be tamed or ashamed of, especially the furious, indignant kind that fractures your calm exterior. But here, in these moments, your body is telling you something different. The heat in your chest, the tightening in your psoas, the restless tension in your levator scapulae are not signs of failure; they are the body’s language of mourning. As Karla McLaren reminds us, anger is a messenger, not an adversary. It carries the message that something precious has been taken, that a boundary has been crossed—by time, by illness, by the slow erosion of vitality.

This anger is not a judgment on your past self but a raw, aching acknowledgment of what’s been lost. It is the body’s rebellion against invisibility, against being forgotten in the narrative of who you are now. You may catch it flickering in the mirror, a flash of resentment toward the person who didn’t guard themselves well enough, who took their health for granted. But this is not cruelty; it is biology. Your body remembers every misstep, every ignored ache, every moment of oblivious ease. The jaw remembers clenching against stress you didn’t yet understand. The diaphragm knows the breath you once took for granted. This anger is the body’s way of saying, “I see you. I miss you. And I am not okay.”

In the stillness of the night, when the world has gone quiet and your body’s whispers grow loud, this anger surfaces—not as a flaw, but as a fierce, necessary witness to your becoming.

## The Illusion of the ‘Healthy’ Before

You think back to that time before sickness settled in, the “healthy” you who seemed to move through days without pause, without complaint. But this memory is a story your mind tells itself—a story that smooths over the knots, the silent screams, the muscle clenches you never noticed. Bessel van der Kolk, in his meticulous work with trauma’s imprint on the body, insists that trauma is not just a psychological event but a physiological one. It lodges in the fascia, the connective tissues, the muscles, and the autonomic nervous system. Your body was keeping score long before the diagnosis, long before you were forced to reckon with what fell apart.

Remember the masseter, the jaw muscle that clenched so tightly you might have woken up with a dull ache behind your ears. It was the first place your fury tucked itself away, a secret you carried in the grinding of teeth during restless nights. The tension there was the body's way of holding back words that could not be spoken, rage that had no safe outlet. The trapezius and levator scapulae, those muscles at the back and sides of your neck, were perpetually contracted, raising your shoulders in subtle, unconscious defense. This wasn’t just tiredness; it was the body bracing for impact, anticipating threat even when none was visible.

Breathing—how shallow it was. Your diaphragm barely moved, restricted by the weight of unacknowledged emotions pressing down on your chest. The breath became a whisper, a faint signal interrupted by the habitual constriction of the ribcage. This is not the breath of ease, but the breath of survival, the breath of someone preparing for the next blow that never came. Van der Kolk reminds us that trauma lives in the body’s inability to complete the defensive responses it needs: fight, flight, or freeze. Instead, the body settles into a chronic state of tension, a quiet, relentless holding pattern.

The psoas, that deep core muscle often called the “muscle of the soul,” was likely clenched tight, a harbinger of the anxiety and stress you dismissed as just part of life. It held the burden of unprocessed fear, a constant low-level contraction that narrowed your experience of safety and freedom. You moved through your days carrying this invisible load, thinking you were fine because the medical charts said so, because you could still show up. But your body knew the truth. The illusion of the “healthy” self was never really whole; it was a facade stitched together by denial and exhaustion.

This is not pathology, this is biology. Your body was not simply waiting to break; it was signaling, trying to communicate the cost of the life you were living. The locked jaw, the shallow breathing, the clenched psoas—they were all messages in a language few learn to read. Before sickness, your body was already writing a story of survival, a story that demands your attention now more than ever.

## The Betrayal of Ignorance

You feel it every time your shoulders pinch upward, the trapezius muscles tightening like a clenched fist beneath your skin, the levator scapulae pulling your neck into a rigid, uncomfortable hold. It’s the weight of fury aimed squarely at the woman you used to be—the one who didn’t know what was coming, who walked blindly into the storm of illness without armor or warning. This anger is not just a mental judgment; it is a physical load, a heaviness that settles deep into the connective tissue, a knot of betrayal and frustration that you carry even in the quietest hours of the night.

You’re angry at her for not having seen the signs, for ignoring the subtle shivers of the psoas before the full collapse, for pushing past the diaphragm’s whispered pleas for breath. It’s the fury of hindsight, the kind that gnaws at the back of your neck, the tightness in the muscles that cradle your head, reminding you with every small movement that you were unprepared, unarmed, vulnerable. And yet, this anger is not the whole story. Underneath it, layered and muffled, is grief—deep, raw, and unacknowledged.

Gabor Maté’s work comes to mind here, his insistence that suppressed emotions don’t vanish; they fossilize inside the body, becoming the very soil from which illness grows. What you might call stubborn disease is often the body’s final, desperate language, speaking in symptoms and chronic pain because it was never given permission to speak in tears or rage. Your anger at your past self is tangled with this grief: the mourning for lost health, lost time, lost versions of yourself. It’s a mourning that the trapezius and levator scapulae bear silently, tightening whenever you forget to breathe fully, whenever exhaustion drapes over you like a wet, heavy cloak at 3 a.m.

This is not failure. This is biology. Your body remembers the shock of betrayal, the moment your cells registered that the story you’d been living was about to shatter. Each tight muscle fiber is a thread in the tapestry of your emotional history, woven with strands of anger that mask the more vulnerable thread of sorrow. You might notice the scent of your skin in these moments—iron and salt, the unmistakable smell of adrenaline kept too long in stasis, the subtle musk of cortisol’s residue. It is the language of the body, unspoken but unmistakable, demanding recognition.

In this tension, you’re invited to notice the difference between fury that burns and fury that protects. The anger at your past self is a fierce sentinel guarding the grief you weren’t ready to face—yet. It is the body’s way of saying, “Hold on. There is more here. Don’t look away.” This is the beginning of compassionate witnessing, where you can begin to unravel the muscle memory of betrayal and open space for the sorrow beneath. The trapezius may still clench, but now it holds a story, and you are learning to listen.

## Herbal Support for the Grieving Liver

In Traditional Chinese Medicine, the liver is the organ of anger—not in the judgmental, “you’re too angry” way you might have heard in a therapist’s office, but as the biological center where your fury first coils and rises. It’s not just metaphor; the liver stores and regulates the smooth flow of qi, your vital energy, and when grief or unresolved rage lodges there, the body stiffens like a clenched masseter or a locked trapezius. The liver’s fascia tightens around the ribs, the diaphragm resists its usual undulating breath, and you feel it somewhere acidic, maybe a bitter taste lingering at the back of your throat or a tightness just below the right rib cage. This is not pathology, this is biology.

Supporting this grieving liver requires more than willpower or affirmations—it demands herbs that speak the language of this ancient organ, working through bitterness and movement to open what has become stuck. Milk Thistle (Silybum marianum) is a time-honored ally here. When you take it, the tincture tastes unexpectedly sweet beneath its initial bitterness, like deep earth softened by rain. It has a resinous scent, almost piney, with a hint of toasted nuts, and when you swallow the drops, you feel a slow unfolding in the upper right quadrant of your abdomen, a subtle loosening as if the liver’s fascia is stretching after a long winter’s cramp. The cells seem to breathe more easily, and the stubborn bile flow that once felt like a locked valve begins to ease.

Dandelion root, often dismissed as a mere weed, carries the true medicine of the liver’s grief. It tastes like a forest floor after rain—dark, slightly astringent, with a whisper of tannins that dry your mouth just enough to signal the start of detoxification. The smell is earthy and raw, not prettied up, grounding you in the reality of the body’s need to purge and repair. When you sip a decoction of dandelion root, you can feel the warmth travel down into your psoas, that deep core muscle that holds your posture and your past. It’s a slow heat, not burning but steady, like a slow fire that kindles clarity in the midst of foggy resentment.

For many women who carry historical anger—anger not just at others but at the person they were before sickness took hold—these herbs are not gentle comforts but fierce midwives. I often recommend a tincture like [Gaia Herbs SleepThru (60 caps)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B004GJYTF8?tag=spankyspinola-20), a blend that combines Milk Thistle, Dandelion, and Bupleurum (Chai Hu), which is a TCM staple for soothing liver qi stagnation. Bupleurum adds a slightly licorice-like sweetness to the bitter profile, and its scent has that unmistakable herbal sharpness that wakes the senses. Taking this tincture feels like a ritual of reclamation—one that honors the body’s wisdom as it processes the grief lodged in the liver’s soft tissues.

In the quiet hours of the night, when the liver’s energy is at its peak, these herbs become more than medicine. They are your companions in the long reckoning with the self you lost, the fury you were taught to hide, and the slow, unfolding release that comes from listening—not to the noise of shame, but to the body’s own language of healing. The liver remembers. The jaw remembers. The body knows how to let go, one bitter sip at a time.

## The Somatic Practice of Forgiving the Ignorant Body: A Short Embodied Practice

You might notice, as you settle into this moment, that the anger toward the person you were before you got sick isn’t just a concept lodged in your mind—it’s a living, pulsing presence in your body. It’s not just a memory; it’s a clenched muscle, a tight fascia, a breath caught somewhere between your diaphragm and your gut. Maybe it’s that stubborn knot in the solar plexus, that prickling fire beneath the ribs where your nervous system held its breath, trying to protect you with the only tools it knew. This is not a failure. This is biology. This is the body’s language, and it’s as honest as the pounding of rain on a tin roof at 3 a.m.

Find a place where you can sit quietly, ideally where the air smells a little like earth or cedar, something grounded and real. Close your eyes gently. Begin by tuning into your breath—the inhale, the exhale, the pause that hovers between. Don’t try to change the breath; just notice it. Feel the diaphragm contract, the belly rise, the ribs expand and then soften. Allow your attention to drift slowly down into the torso, into the territory where this anger lives. Maybe it’s a hard clench in the belly, or a tightness wrapped around the lower ribs. Maybe it’s a flickering heat or a dull, unyielding ache. Wherever it is, bring a soft, curious attention there.

Now, without judgment or the impulse to fix or soothe, breathe into that place. Imagine the breath as a gentle tide, washing over the muscle or fascia, the nerve endings waking to the sensation of air and movement. This is not about erasing the anger or pushing it away. It’s about acknowledging that the body that held this anger was doing its best. The diaphragm, the psoas, the vagus nerve—they were all trying to keep you safe with the nervous system you had at the time. Thich Nhat Hanh teaches us to “hold the hurt with tenderness,” and this is your chance to do just that with your own body.

Say quietly to yourself, “I see you. I see the anger you carried. You did the best you could.” Let these words sink into muscle and fascia, into the soft tissues that remember what the mind has forgotten. This is not about excusing harm or forgetting pain. It’s about recognizing the limits of the nervous system then, and offering a somatic bow to the body that kept you alive, even when it felt ignorant or unkind.

Sit here for a few breaths longer, breathing into the place where anger lives, honoring the biology of survival. When you’re ready, gently open your eyes. The jaw might unclench a little, the shoulders might drop, the breath might feel a shade freer. This is not a quick fix; it’s a practice, a quiet, grounded ritual of forgiveness rooted in flesh and bone. You are not separate from the body that once held your fury—you are the whole, complex messenger, and the body remembers.

## Integration: Moving from Anger to Fierce Compassion for the Person You Were

You sit in the quiet that follows the storm of your fury, the late afternoon light spilling through the window, warming the nape of your neck. The masseter muscle, once clenched so tight it felt like a steel trap around your jaw, begins to soften. You notice this release—how the tension that held your teeth in a relentless grind finally yields, allowing a small, involuntary sigh to escape your lips. This is the beginning of integration, the slow, deliberate shift from the raw, burning anger at the person you were before you got sick, to a fierce, unyielding compassion for her. It is a biological transition, a recalibration of the nervous system that happens not in the mind, but in the fascia, the muscles, the very cells of your body.

The anger you felt was not a mistake; it was a necessary fire, a signal that a boundary had been crossed, a loss mourned. But as you sit here, feeling the subtle shift in your body, you realize that the illness forced a boundary the past self couldn't set. The body, in its infinite, brutal wisdom, demanded a halt when the mind would not. The psoas, that deep, primal muscle of survival, which had been coiled tight with unacknowledged fear and exhaustion, begins to unfurl. You feel it deep in your pelvis, a slow, aching release that speaks of surrender, not defeat. This is not pathology, this is biology. The body is finally allowing itself to rest, to acknowledge the weight it has been carrying.

This compassion is not a soft, yielding thing. It is fierce. It is the kind of compassion that looks unflinchingly at the ignorance of the past self and says, "You did not know, but you did your best." It is the compassion that honors the body's desperate attempts to keep you safe, even when those attempts looked like tension, like illness, like failure. As Pema Chödrön teaches, compassion is not a relationship between the healer and the wounded; it's a relationship between equals. You are learning to be an equal with your past self, to hold her ignorance and her struggle with the same reverence you hold your current pain.

You feel this integration in the breath. The diaphragm, which had been locked in a shallow, guarded rhythm, begins to move more freely. The breath deepens, expanding the ribcage, filling the lungs with a sense of spaciousness that has been absent for so long. The levator scapulae, the muscles that have held your shoulders in a perpetual shrug of defense, finally drop. The physical release of this realization is profound. It is the body's way of saying, "We are safe now. We can let go."

This is the work of the somatic guide, the herbalist, the woman who refuses to see her fury as ugly. It is the work of honoring the body's messengers, of listening to the jaw, the psoas, the diaphragm, and allowing them to lead you back to yourself. The anger was the messenger, but the compassion is the medicine. And as you sit in the fading light, feeling the softening of your own flesh, you know that this medicine is taking root, deep in the biology of your being.

## FAQ: Navigating the Anger at Your Past Self

**Q: Is it normal to feel so much rage toward the person I was before I got sick?**

**A:** Yes. The body knows when a boundary has been crossed, even if that boundary was crossed by your own past ignorance. This rage is not a moral failure; it is a biological response to profound loss. When you look back and see the ways you pushed through exhaustion, ignored the tightening of your psoas, or dismissed the shallow breathing of your diaphragm, the anger that rises is the body’s way of demanding acknowledgment. It is a fierce, protective energy that says, “We deserved better.” This is not pathology, this is biology. The fury is a messenger, signaling that you are finally ready to grieve the health and the naiveté you lost.

**Q: Where does this specific kind of anger live in the body, and how can I recognize it?**

**A:** Anger at the past self often lodges in the muscles of defense and holding. You might feel it in the masseter, the jaw muscle that clenches when words of regret or frustration are held back. It frequently settles in the trapezius and levator scapulae, pulling your shoulders up toward your ears in a perpetual, unconscious brace against the past. You may also feel it deep in the gut or the liver, where Traditional Chinese Medicine tells us unresolved anger and grief stagnate. It can manifest as a sudden, smoldering heat behind the sternum at 3 AM, or a tight, coiled sensation in the pelvis. Recognizing it means tuning into these specific somatic details without trying to immediately fix them.

**Q: How do I process this anger without feeling overwhelmed by guilt?**

**A:** Guilt is often the mind’s way of trying to control what the body is already feeling. To process the anger without guilt, you must move out of the narrative of blame and into the biology of sensation. When the anger rises, notice where it lives—perhaps the tightening of the diaphragm or the heat in the chest. Breathe into that space, acknowledging that the person you were did the best she could with the nervous system she had. As Resmaa Menakem teaches, healing happens in the body, not just in the mind. Offer your past self fierce compassion, recognizing that she was operating without the wisdom your illness has since forced upon you. The jaw remembers, but it can also learn to soften.

**Q: Can herbs really help with this kind of emotional processing?**

**A:** Absolutely. Herbs are serious medicine, and they interact directly with the body’s physiological responses to emotion. When anger and grief are lodged in the liver, causing stagnation and physical tension, herbs like Milk Thistle and Dandelion root can support the organ’s ability to process and release. These are not just gentle comforts; they are fierce allies that stimulate bile flow, ease the fascia around the ribs, and help move the heavy, stuck energy of historical anger. Taking a targeted tincture, such as [Gaia Herbs Adrenal Health Nightly Restore (60 caps)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B01DA54ESK?tag=spankyspinola-20), can be a profound somatic ritual, engaging the senses of taste and smell to ground you in the present moment while the body does the deep work of letting go.
